headline: Oil Shock and Software Resilience Split a Fractured Friday Session
body_html:
Midday Friday, the market is holding a tentative advance, with advancing issues leading decliners 288 to 209 across the 501-name universe. The dominant theme is unmistakable: a geopolitical oil shock driven by U.S. military strikes against Iran — now in their thirteenth consecutive night — that has pushed crude above $100 a barrel and sent Treasury yields retreating as bond markets grapple with competing inflation and flight-to-safety pressures. The ten-year yield is hovering near levels last seen in January 2025.

Sector leadership is sharply bifurcated. Software stands alone at the top, up roughly 1%, while Energy follows with a gain near half a percent. Healthcare and Homebuilders are modestly positive. On the losing end, Semiconductors are under meaningful pressure, shedding more than 1.6%, with freight and logistics names compounding the industrial sector's drag. The standout mover of the session is Schlumberger, surging nearly 7% as oilfield-services demand assumptions reprice alongside crude. Equinix and Edwards Lifesciences each gained more than 5%, adding breadth to the day's winners.

Into the close, traders will be monitoring any fresh developments in the Strait of Hormuz, where ship transits have held steady at three for three consecutive days but remain a flashpoint. A sustained move in crude or a shift in that corridor's traffic could redraw positioning before the weekend.

briefs.headline: Schlumberger Surges as Oilfield-Services Demand Gets Repriced
briefs.body: Schlumberger leads the entire universe Friday, climbing nearly 7% intraday. The move reflects a rapid reassessment of oilfield-services demand as crude pushes above $100 amid Middle East escalation. Investors appear to be pricing in a sustained period of elevated upstream activity should supply-route disruptions persist.

briefs.headline: Semiconductor Names Bear the Brunt of the Risk-Off Rotation
briefs.body: The semiconductor sector is the session's worst performer, down more than 1.6%. MaxLinear is the sharpest individual decliner, falling over 13%, while Credo Technology shed nearly 6%. The pressure reflects both profit-taking and concern that energy-cost inflation tied to the Hormuz disruption could squeeze margins across the chip supply chain.

briefs.headline: Freight Brokers Hit Hard as Rerouting Costs Mount
briefs.body: RXO Inc and C.H. Robinson are among the session's steepest decliners, dropping 9% and nearly 7% respectively. Reporting that oil tankers are spending an extra month at sea and more than two million dollars per voyage to avoid Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb has raised fears that broader freight economics are deteriorating rapidly.

briefs.headline: Treasury Yields Pull Back Even as Oil Stokes Inflation Concerns
briefs.body: The ten-year Treasury yield is retreating Friday, hovering near its highest levels since January 2025, as bond markets weigh inflation risk from surging oil against a flight-to-safety bid. The tension between those two forces is creating unusual cross-asset volatility, with equities and fixed income moving in conflicting directions simultaneously.

briefs.headline: Equinix Jumps More Than 5% as Data-Center Demand Narrative Holds
briefs.body: The data-center REIT posted one of the session's strongest individual gains, rising more than 5% midday. The move suggests investors continue to view digital infrastructure as insulated from the macro disruption roiling energy and logistics names, even as reports surface of an AI-platform security breach prompting legislative action in Washington.

briefs.headline: Asian Markets Sank Overnight as Oil Spike Amplified Rate Anxiety
briefs.body: Asian equities fell broadly in overnight trading as crude's surge above $100 reignited concerns that central banks may have less room to ease than previously anticipated. Bond markets in the region also struggled, with the dual pressure of higher energy costs and geopolitical uncertainty weighing on risk appetite well before Wall Street opened Friday.
